Introduction¶

In this notebook, we will do a comprehensive analysis of the Android app market by comparing thousands of apps in the Google Play store.

About the Dataset of Google Play Store Apps & Reviews¶

Data Source:
App and review data was scraped from the Google Play Store by Lavanya Gupta in 2018. Original files listed here.

Import Statements¶

Notebook Presentation¶

Read the Dataset¶

Numeric Type Conversion: Examine the Number of Installs¶

Challenge: How many apps had over 1 billion (that's right - BILLION) installations? How many apps just had a single install?

Check the datatype of the Installs column.

Count the number of apps at each level of installations.

Convert the number of installations (the Installs column) to a numeric data type. Hint: this is a 2-step process. You'll have make sure you remove non-numeric characters first.

Challenge: How many apps had over 1 billion (that's right - BILLION) installations? How many apps just had a single install?

Check the datatype of the Installs column.

Count the number of apps at each level of installations.

Convert the number of installations (the Installs column) to a numeric data type. Hint: this is a 2-step process. You'll have make sure you remove non-numeric characters first.

Find the Most Expensive Apps, Filter out the Junk, and Calculate a (ballpark) Sales Revenue Estimate¶

Let's examine the Price column more closely.

Challenge: Convert the price column to numeric data. Then investigate the top 20 most expensive apps in the dataset.

Remove all apps that cost more than $250 from the df_apps_clean DataFrame.

Add a column called 'Revenue_Estimate' to the DataFrame. This column should hold the price of the app times the number of installs. What are the top 10 highest grossing paid apps according to this estimate? Out of the top 10 highest grossing paid apps, how many are games?

Challenge: Can you create this chart with the Series containing the genre data?

Try experimenting with the built in colour scales in Plotly. You can find a full list here.

  • Find a way to set the colour scale using the color_continuous_scale parameter.
  • Find a way to make the color axis disappear by using coloraxis_showscale.

You'll want to use the df_free_vs_paid DataFrame that you created above that has the total number of free and paid apps per category.

See if you can figure out how to get the look above by changing the categoryorder to 'total descending' as outlined in the documentation here here.

Plotly Box Plots: Lost Downloads for Paid Apps¶

Challenge: Create a box plot that shows the number of Installs for free versus paid apps. How does the median number of installations compare? Is the difference large or small?

Use the Box Plots Guide and the .box API reference to create the following chart.

Plotly Box Plots: Revenue by App Category¶

Challenge: See if you can generate the chart below:

Looking at the hover text, how much does the median app earn in the Tools category? If developing an Android app costs $30,000 or thereabouts, does the average photography app recoup its development costs?

Hint: I've used 'min ascending' to sort the categories.

How Much Can You Charge? Examine Paid App Pricing Strategies by Category¶

Challenge: What is the median price price for a paid app? Then compare pricing by category by creating another box plot. But this time examine the prices (instead of the revenue estimates) of the paid apps. I recommend using {categoryorder':'max descending'} to sort the categories.

2.99

Opportunity Score by Category¶

Business question: Which app categories have high install volume but few competing apps — and which are so crowded that demand per app is negligible?

Opportunity Score = total installs ÷ number of apps. A high score signals undersupplied demand; a low score signals saturation.

Key finding: The three highest-opportunity categories (shown above) combine massive total install volumes with a relatively small number of competing apps — meaning each app in those categories captures far more demand than average. The three most saturated categories have the inverse profile: a large number of apps competing for proportionally modest install volume, making differentiation the primary challenge for any new entrant.

Paid App Viability by Category¶

Business question: In which categories do paid apps generate meaningful revenue — and in which are free apps so dominant that charging is commercially unviable?

Filter: paid apps only, price ≤ $29.99 (removes data-entry junk without an arbitrary blanket cap). Minimum 10 paid apps per category to ensure statistical credibility.

Key finding: Only a subset of categories sustain paid apps at meaningful revenue levels. Categories where paid apps are viable tend to serve professional or niche audiences willing to pay for specific utility — confirmed by median prices sitting above the overall paid-app median of $2.99. Categories dominated by casual or general-purpose apps show near-zero median revenue estimates even among paid entries, indicating that free-with-ads is the only commercially rational model there.

K-Means App Segmentation with PCA¶

Business question: What distinct market positions exist in the Play Store — and where does a new app fit based on its product characteristics?

Cluster 8,199 individual apps (not categories) on five normalised features: log(Installs+1), Rating, log(Reviews+1), Price, and Size_MBs. StandardScaler normalisation is required — raw install counts would dominate all other features.

Key findings:

  • The largest segment by app count is Mass Market Free — high install volume, free pricing, broad category spread.
  • A new developer targeting sustainable early traction should focus on Mass Market Free: the segment combines the highest average installs with zero price friction, giving the best chance of organic discovery. Premium Paid requires an established reputation or a niche with proven willingness to pay.
  • Free vs paid does not split cleanly across all segments — Hidden Gems contains mostly free apps with above-average ratings, suggesting quality and niche focus matter more than monetisation model alone.
  • PCA explains meaningful variance on PC1 (driven by install/review volume) and PC2 (driven by rating and price). The four clusters are visually separable in this two-component projection, validating the k=4 choice.

Key Findings¶

Data scope: 8,199 Android apps from the Google Play Store after cleaning and deduplication.

Content ratings: Over 80% of apps are rated Everyone, confirming the Play Store is dominated by general-audience apps.

Category competition: Games and Family are the most crowded categories by app count. Communication and Video Players lead by total installs.

Opportunity Score: Categories with the highest installs-per-app ratio are undersupplied relative to demand — these represent the clearest market entry opportunity for a new developer.

Paid app viability: After filtering to apps priced ≤ $29.99 with at least 10 paid apps per category, only a subset of categories sustain meaningful median revenue estimates. Professional tools and niche productivity categories outperform casual and entertainment categories.

K-Means segmentation (k=4): Four distinct market positions emerge from clustering on log(Installs), Rating, log(Reviews), Price, and Size: Mass Market Free (high volume, free), Premium Paid (low volume, high price, high rating), Hidden Gems (moderate installs, high rating, low reviews), and Struggling (low installs, low rating). Mass Market Free is the largest segment and the recommended entry point for new developers given its install potential with no price friction.

PCA: Two principal components capture meaningful variance. PC1 is driven by install and review volume; PC2 by rating and price. The four segments are visually separable in PCA space, validating the cluster structure.
